title: Using h-Cobordisms to Detect Non-Trivial Homotopy Groups in Spaces of Legendrian
speaker: Thomas Kragh
abstract: In this talk I will first define the space of h-cobordisms associated to a manifold M. This space is known to have many non-trivial homotopy groups and in stable range (they can often be computed using Waldhausen's algebraic K-theory of spaces). I will then define maps from these spaces into certain spaces of Legendrians, and I will describe how we detect that these maps are to some extend non-trivial on homotopy groups. A special case is that we essentially (and in stable range) find two copies of the h-cobordism space of a point inside the space of Legendrians of the jet 1 bundle of the n-sphere based at a standard Legendrian unknot (the Whitney sphere).

proof_stages.label: Partition and h-cobordism source spaces
proof_stages.summary: Partitions of M are modeled as functions M x D^1 -> D^1 that equal the projection near the boundary and have 0 as a regular value, with the h-cobordism space singled out by requiring the negative sublevel set to be an h-cobordism.

proof_stages.label: Type-I map by moving a fold
proof_stages.summary: Near a codimension-one submanifold where a Legendrian front has the standard fold form, the fold parameter is replaced by a partition function and the result is extended by the original Legendrian, producing a family in the Legendrian space.

proof_stages.label: Type-II map by moving a directed conormal
proof_stages.summary: In a cosphere chart where the Legendrian agrees with the directed conormal of M x {0}, the construction replaces it by the directed conormal of the zero set of a partition function, and this can be combined with the moving-fold family in a larger cosphere contact manifold.

proof_stages.label: Generating-function lift of the moving-fold map
proof_stages.summary: After defining quadratic- or linear-at-infinity generating functions, the speaker shows that when a one-auxiliary-variable generating function has standard fold normal form, replacing the fold coordinate by a partition function lifts the type-I Legendrian family to a family with generating functions.

proof_stages.label: Stable h-cobordism machinery
proof_stages.summary: Positive and negative stabilizations of partitions and generating functions are matched, stable h-cobordism spaces are formed, and Igusa- and Waldhausen-type connectivity theorems provide highly connected maps in the stable range.

proof_stages.label: Type-I detection by the difference function
proof_stages.summary: The difference function of a stabilized generating function defines a detection map to stable partition or h-cobordism space, and its composition with the moving-fold family agrees, up to the stated caveats, with the stable map induced by inclusion, forcing nontrivial Legendrian homotopy classes because these classes do not come from the generating-function fiber.

proof_stages.label: Type-II detection by generating hypersurfaces
proof_stages.summary: Generating hypersurfaces are introduced as the cosphere-bundle analogue of generating functions, their stabilization and fibration properties are invoked, and the conormal-moving family is detected by a stabilization map whose expected fiber behavior gives rational injectivity in the cases under discussion.

proof_stages.label: Complementarity of the two constructions
proof_stages.summary: Using the embedding J^1 R^n into S^*R^{n+1} and the contactomorphism with J^1 S^n, the type-I family is shown to survive under its detector while the type-II detector vanishes on that factor, so the two h-cobordism families give complementary homotopy classes.

examples.name: Rank-jump constructible sheaf for a directed conormal
examples.note: The speaker describes the conormal of Q x {0} as the microsupport of a sheaf that is rank zero on one side and rank one on the other, giving a sheaf-theoretic visualization of the conormal construction without using it in the proof.
examples.engagement_reason: He describes the rank-zero/rank-one jump sheaf as microlocal intuition for a directed conormal but does not compute or develop the sheaf example.
